data.title: VIU:CA: Cleanest Way To Play Developed (Ex-NA) Equity In CAD
data.description:
VIU:CA Overview

The Vanguard FTSE Developed All Cap ex North America Index ETF (VIU:CA) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) that invests in global stocks, excluding U.S. and Canada. This is a fund I own personally as a core sleeve of my portfolio, and for good reason — it's the cleanest and most efficient way for Canadian investors to get local developed-market exposure across Japan, the U.K., Europe, Korea, and Australia, without paying a premium for it.

VIU:CA's benchmark target is the FTSE Developed All Cap ex North America Index, and while it doesn't have a ton of direct competitors in Canada, I will compare it against its closest peer, the iShares Core MSCI EAFE IMI Index ETF (XEF:CA), another popular Canadian option for the same space. The key distinction worth noting upfront is that VIU:CA tracks the FTSE Developed All Cap index, which includes small-cap stocks, giving it a slightly broader mandate compared to the MSCI EAFE IMI methodology that XEF:CA uses. While the correlation between the two funds is pretty high VIU:CA's marginal edge in breadth, and its slightly different geographic construction make it my preferred vehicle in this space.

After a strong 2025, the fund is up further year-to-date in 2026, and with geopolitical tensions showing early signs of easing — notably the potential end to the war in the Middle East — international equities could have significant upside ahead. If you are a Canadian investor who has been running a U.S.-heavy equity portfolio and wants to ensure you have exposure to local developed economies worldwide, VIU:CA makes sense.

Fund Breakdown

VIU:CA was launched on December 1, 2015, by Vanguard Investments Canada Inc. with a mandate to track the performance of broad global equity focused on developed markets, excluding the U.S. and Canada. The fund invests directly or indirectly in large-, mid-, and small-cap stocks of companies located in developed markets worldwide. The fund has a daily volume of about 241,000 shares traded, worth roughly $11MM CAD per day, which is solid for a Canadian-listed ETF. I've written about liquidity concerns with many Canadian ETFs before, but VIU:CA has no such issue. Institutions can size into and out of positions without blowing up the bid-ask spread, and retail investors can execute at any time during market hours. The fund also distributes quarterly, which is a nice touch for income-oriented investors who want some natural return of cash while maintaining the growth profile.

To be explicit on the peer comparison: VIU:CA is up ~10% year-to-date in 2026 versus XEF:CA's ~7%, a meaningful gap that I attribute largely to VIU:CA's inclusion of South Korean names like Samsung and SK Hynix, which XEF:CA excludes given MSCI's classification of Korea as an emerging market. In a world where U.S. equity valuations remain stretched — the S&P 500 still trades above 25x forward earnings — international developed markets at a significant discount are simply a better risk-adjusted proposition right now. Baird Asset Management noted in January 2026 that the current phase of international strength should persist "supported by more attractive relative valuations, a softer U.S. dollar, and broadening global earnings growth", which I think is very well said, and VIU:CA captures that thesis more fully than XEF:CA does.

The fund employs a 0.23% yearly MER (management fee of 0.20%), which is similar to XEF:CA, with a comparable MER of 0.22%. The fund has been trading for years, with a NAV of $8.9Bn+ and is a well known Vanguard offering. Over the past few months, even before the war started, a broadening in the market was taking place - AI had driven multiples too high and value was winning to start 2026. And amidst geopolitical uncertainty, I prefer having exposure to global stocks around 10-15x earnings with stable cash flows vs. AI hype as oil fields get bombed in the middle east. And versus its peers, its continued to stand alone a top this mandate, giving me some comfort amidst a chaotic and volatile stock market.

Fund Portfolio & Rebalancing Profile

When comparing VIU:CA portfolio to peers, there are some differences in the portfolio breakdown from a geographic perspective. Vanguard counts South Korea as a developed market, so you get exposure to Samsung and other tech players in this fund. Additionally, the fund has slightly higher weights in consumer discretionary and energy - both of which I find as a plus. I think the high end consumer will generally not be affected by the energy crisis and spending should stabilize - and on the other side, energy prices won't drop any time soon (I doubt WTI will go below $80 this year), meaning both mutually exclusive sectors should outperform, even though consumers as a whole (notably in the bottom of the K shape) may struggle.

Additionally, the fund has a higher weighting in small caps versus XEF:CA, with about 15% of the fund in mid and small caps. This is where some torque can occur if the ceasefire holds, and what I think should give some alpha in this sleeve as well.

Risks

From a risk perspective, a beta of 1.07 for VIU:CA notes that the fund has a bit more volatility as the benchmark. This is a bit surprising given that there's less technology and other high growth stocks, but with the fund's recent outperformance and additional geopolitical volatility, this makes sense. Additionally, when evaluating the fund's risk-adjusted returns, I analyzed the Sharpe ratio to see whether the fund has generated excess returns over the risk-free rate when adjusted for total volatility. The Sharpe ratio for the fund is 1.02, above the score of 1+ to prove strong risk-adjusted returns, and above the category average of 0.82, which I find impressive.

From a macroeconomic perspective, another risk here is a strong Canadian dollar. All of VIU:CA's underlying holdings are priced outside Canada, meaning that if the CAD appreciates significantly against the yen, euro, sterling, or Korean won, reported returns in CAD will be reduced. The fund is unhedged, and if Canadian dollar strength is not a near-term concern (and given trade uncertainty with the U.S., I'd argue the CAD has limited material upside from here), staying unhedged makes sense.

The other risk is geopolitical. The conflict in the Middle East has created disruptions in energy markets, and European and Japanese economies are more exposed to imported energy shocks than the U.S. market is. A prolonged conflict could weigh on European and Japanese industrial activity. That said, the flip side is the opportunity - if the ceasefire holds and oil prices stabilize, international equities that have been discounting geopolitical risk could see a meaningful re-rating upward.

Additionally, CUSMA negotiations in the summer remain an overhang on global trade sentiment, and any escalation in tariff rhetoric from the U.S. administration could create a risk-off episode in developed market equities that export to the U.S.. That said, VIU:CA's broad diversification across 3,655 names in over a dozen countries limits single-name and single-country blow-up risk considerably.

Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that it's the best way to play global (ex-NA) equity in Canada. I rate it a cautious Buy for three specific reasons: firstly, international equities remain attractively valued relative to a stretched U.S. market, with Europe and Japan still trading at significant discounts despite their recent run. Second, VIU:CA's inclusion of South Korea differentiates it from XEF:CA at a moment when Korean tech is a direct beneficiary of AI capital spending broadening beyond U.S. borders. Third, the fund's small-cap tilt (~15% mid and small caps) gives it additional alpha potential if a ceasefire materializes and risk appetite recovers in developed markets. The main risks are the unhedged currency exposure — CAD strength would be a headwind — and the ongoing geopolitical and tariff uncertainty, which can create sharp volatility in international equity broadly. But in my opinion, is that it's the best way to play global (ex-NA) equity in Canada. It's a fund that I own, and given the current economy, I think it's worth a cautious Buy.

data.title: Chegg: Despite Activist Investor, Don't Count On A Successful Spinoff
data.description:
In a surprising turn for the stock markets, the S&P 500 has soared back near all-time highs. Investors are taking a risk-on attitude again, even with the conflict in Iran largely unresolved.

Amidst this turn in risk appetite, small-cap stocks have also been rallying meaningfully, including stocks that the market had largely left for dead, like Chegg (CHGG). Since bottoming in March around $0.60 per share, Chegg has nearly doubled. The question for investors is: Can this rebound rally be sustained?

I last wrote a bearish article on Chegg in November, when the stock was trading at similar levels near $1. This was shortly after the company decided to restructure its business into two units: the legacy Academic Services segment, which offered test-bank services, and higher-growing Chegg Skilling segment (which consists of Duolingo language-learning competitor Busuu as well an AI skills bootcamp-oriented program). Despite placing the spotlight on Skilling's growth, I find it unlikely that Chegg can materially rally from here; that said, if activists succeed in a spinoff or sale in Chegg, there could be upside. I'm moderating my outlook on the stock to "Neutral."

First, let's address the principal reason that shares of Chegg have skyrocketed over the past month. In early April, a small activist investor fund called Galloway Capital Management announced a stake worth ~5.4% of Chegg's stock (currently worth about ~$6 million). In an open letter to Chegg's management, the firm suggested that Chegg formally split its business into the two units it created last year, Academic Services and Skilling. The rationale is clear: to separate the legacy, declining Academic Services business, which is plagued by concerns that AI has entirely displaced its services, away from the double-digit growth in the Skilling segment. As a reminder, last year Chegg engaged Goldman Sachs (GS) to explore "strategic options." No sale materialized from this, and Chegg opted to remain a public company that split into these two segments. The Galloway news has rekindled hopes that Chegg might pursue strategic options again.

In Chegg's most recent quarter, the Skilling grew at a 10% y/y growth rate to $17.7 million in revenue (which slightly missed the company's rounded estimate of an $18 million revenue for Q4), while the rest of the business declined 48% y/y.

My previous article posited that Chegg's segment restructuring was merely putting lipstick on the pig (Skilling itself is a strange combination of two unrelated products that happen not to be suffering revenue declines), but given Galloway's assertion that the two businesses are worth more standalone, let's attempt to derive an independent valuation of the two segments.

Sum of the parts valuation doesn't yield much upside for Chegg

Now, before we dig into the details of valuation, note that there's one complication: though Chegg reports revenue split between its two segments, it does not allocate expenses between them, and such we have to use our own estimates of EBITDA between the two segments.

We'll use the most aggressive valuation methodology here, to illustrate that there's not much upside to Chegg even if it were to attempt a spinoff:

  • Value the Chegg Skilling growth business on a revenue multiple
  • Value the legacy Academic Services business on an adjusted EBITDA multiple

For FY26, as shown in the chart above, Chegg has issued very limited guidance. It's merely calling for "double digit" growth for Skilling, including $17.5-$18.0 million in Q1 revenue (8.5%-11.6% y/y growth range, or 10% at the midpoint). Meanwhile, it's expecting $11.5 million in midpoint adjusted EBITDA (across both segments) in Q1, a 17.8% margin against total revenue, and a 40% y/y decline from $19.3 million adjusted EBITDA in Q1'25.

First, let's tackle the Chegg Skilling business. Since the most-monetized product in this segment is the language-learning app Busuu, we think Duolingo (DUOL) is the most instructive comp, at ~3x revenue.

If we assume Chegg Skills retains its 10% y/y growth across the full year, FY26 Skilling revenue would be $75.6 million. We assign a 2x revenue multiple to this business ($151 million enterprise value), at a one-turn discount to Duolingo to account for the fact that Duolingo is guiding to much faster revenue growth (15%-18% y/y) and has proven cash flow.

Meanwhile, if we assume adjusted EBITDA falls 40% y/y for the full year (in line with Q1 guidance), full-year adjusted EBITDA would be $41.1 million. Chegg has suggested that the Academic Services division will remain profitable and cash-flow positive. If we generously assume it will pull in ~75% of total company profit (as it's 75% of guidance revenue in Q1), Academic Services FY26 adjusted EBITDA would be $30.8 million.

Based on other slow-growing, "penalty box" consumer internet/tech stocks that are trading around a ~4x adjusted EBITDA multiple, we assign a similar 4x adjusted EBITDA multiple to Academic Services, giving us a $123 million value for this segment.

Using $151 million in value for Skilling plus $123 million for Academic Services yields a total enterprise value of $274 million for the combined company.

Meanwhile, at current share prices near $1, Chegg trades at a market cap of $114.0 million. Netting off the $85.2 million of cash and $53.8 million of convertible debt on Chegg's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $82.6 million.

This suggests that my aggressive sum-of-the-parts valuation for Chegg has about ~3.3x upside to where the stock is currently trading.

Pitfalls

We broadly agree with Galloway Capital Management's assertion that Chegg can likely unlock value by divorcing its problem-riddled Academic Services segment from the double-digit growth in the Skilling segment. That said, there are many pitfalls that are not factored into my very aggressive sum-of-the-parts valuation:

  • Cost dis-synergies. Chegg is already a micro-cap company as it is. Splitting the business into two segments is likely to burden the company with tremendous administrative expenses that will make even its Q1 profitability estimate, at a 40% y/y decline, difficult to hit. We note as well that transaction expenses/banking fees will eat into any value unlocked by separating the two companies.
  • Academic Services may not stay profitable for long. Guidance for Q1 implies that revenue in this segment will decline 48% y/y. We assigned quite generous profitability and valuation to this segment, but in reality, it's unlikely to find any buyer willing to support a consumer business losing half of its revenue with the opex it needs to survive.
  • Skilling's growth rates may decelerate, and even a ~2x revenue multiple may be too generous. Duolingo's FY26 revenue guidance of 15%-18% y/y represents a substantial deceleration from 39% revenue growth in FY25. Chegg is expecting to hold double-digit revenue growth in FY26, not decelerating at all versus a 9.8% growth pace in FY25. As a lesser-known product, we find this scenario of stable growth rates in a very tough macro environment very unlikely for Chegg, unless its AI bootcamp products really take off (and we note that so far, they haven't yet).

Key takeaways

In my view, the presence of activist support for Chegg is likely to put a floor on the stock price in the near term; hence, my upgrade on the stock to "Neutral." That said, I still have very little confidence in Chegg's ability to stage a turnaround, let alone find support and a willing buyer for either of its businesses in a business climate that is very afraid of the knock-on impacts of AI. Continue to avoid this stock and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Eos Energy: A High-Potential Growth Story With Execution Still To Be Proven
data.description:
Intro

I’ve had Eos Energy Enterprises, Inc. (EOSE) on my radar for years and have always been a fan of their story. In my view, they provide the missing piece in today’s energy landscape: a cost-effective, long-duration storage solution. However, while the technology is promising, the company’s financing history has been challenging.

The financial situation improved in 2024 following the deal with Cerberus, which resolved the critical liquidity issue the company was facing at the time. That said, the deal came with harsh terms, and the risk of common shareholders being wiped out or heavily diluted felt uncomfortably high to me.

After the latest recapitalization round last year in November, the funding situation improved significantly, and with the production ramp‑up, it seemed like an appropriate time to start following the company more closely again. Having postponed a deeper analysis for various reasons a couple of times, I decided to take a closer look now that the company has released its preliminary Q1 FY26 results, which could potentially mark a turning point in the company's not-so-smooth trajectory thus far.

The big question, after the wide miss in last quarter's results and outlook, has become the credibility of management’s forecasts — something we should get more clarity on when the company reports its next quarterly earnings on May 5.

Eos’ Differentiated Storage Solution

Put simply, their energy storage solution offers a much-needed stabilizing bridge for times when the sun isn’t shining, and the wind isn’t blowing—smoothing out the peaks and valleys in power demand. At a more complex level, there are many use cases across the energy value chain where their technology serves as the critical missing link, enabling the entire system to operate with significantly greater efficiency.

Parallels With Tesla Megapack

I think another effective way to explain EOSE to someone less familiar with their story is through the lens of the Tesla Megapack. Beyond the widely circulated photos of fields filled with shipping‑container‑like battery units, many investors have likely heard about the massive growth of Tesla Energy, driven by Megapacks deployed in the field or Powerwalls mounted on garage walls.

In Tesla's January earnings call, Elon Musk discussed the rapid expansion of Tesla Energy, which reported 49% installation growth in 2025, making it the company's fastest-growing segment:

With respect to energy, the Tesla Energy team has done incredible work and the growth rate on that is continuing to be very strong. And we're building more manufacturing capacity and expect that energy will have very high growth for really as far into the future as we can imagine.

The solar opportunity is underestimated. We think the best way to add significant capability to the grid is -- or not only to the grid, let's say, powering AI data centers is solar and batteries on earth and solar in space.

An Eos storage solution is fundamentally comparable to a Tesla Megapack, targeting the same utility-scale market. The primary differentiator lies in the battery chemistry: by relying on more abundant and simpler materials, Eos offers a solution that is more cost-effective to manufacture at scale. While this simplicity results in a different performance profile compared to lithium-ion—typically favoring longer-duration discharge—it provides a clear path to lower long-term storage costs.

Zinc vs. Li-ion Battery Packs: Key Differences

Category

 Zinc‑based Lithium‑ion
Core Materials Zinc, bromine, water‑based electrolyte Lithium, nickel, cobalt, graphite
Safety Non‑flammable chemistry; no thermal runaway Risk of thermal runaway; requires cooling + fire‑suppression systems
Cost Structure Lower material cost; simpler supply chain; cheaper to scale Higher material cost, supply chain volatility, and more complex manufacturing
Round-trip efficiency (RTE) 60-70% 85-90%
Cycle Life Very high cycle life at deep discharge (80–100% DoD) Good cycle life, but degrades faster at high DoD
Energy Density Lower; requires more physical space Higher, more compact installations
Ideal Use Cases Long‑duration storage (6–12+ hours), daily cycling, grid balancing Short‑ to mid‑duration storage (2–4 hours), fast response, high‑power applications
Environmental Impact No lithium, cobalt, or nickel; easier recycling Mining‑intensive materials; recycling is improving but still challenging
Temperature Tolerance Performs well in wide temperature ranges; minimal thermal mgmt needs Sensitive to temperature; requires active thermal management
CapEx / OpEx Profile Lower CapEx per kWh for long‑duration; low OpEx Higher CapEx; higher OpEx due to cooling + degradation
Degradation Behavior Very low degradation over time; stable chemistry; even 20-year warranty is offered. Gradual capacity fade; more pronounced under high‑stress cycling. Normal average lifecycle 10-15 years.
Supply Chain Security Fully produced in the U.S. China-sourced components needed

To sum up, I think these two solutions can be viewed more as complements than competitors in meeting the energy market’s storage needs. I think this is especially true given that the Tesla Megapack has long been touted as unable to keep pace with demand, even though it isn’t always the perfect solution from a cost perspective for every current energy storage application.

Growth Trends in Utility‑Scale Energy Storage

The International Energy Agency (IEA) writes in their Electricity 2026 Analysis and forecast to 2030:

As electricity use grows, power systems will need greater flexibility to securely and cost-effectively integrate an increasingly diverse mix of electricity generation sources while accommodating evolving demand patterns and technologies.

Battery storage has become one of the most versatile tools for providing short term power system flexibility. They can support the integration of wind and solar power by responding quickly to provide system balancing and grid support services, contribute to security of supply through capacity provision, and shift renewable generation to periods of high demand. As a result, they help defer or reduce the need for some network upgrades.

While there’s no 2025 summary available from the IEA, I think the trends are clearly visible from these charts:

VRE - Variable Renewable Energy.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides data on planned additions to utility-scale storage systems in the United States for 2026:

To sum up, I think it’s fair to say that demand for utility-scale storage systems isn’t an issue. Now, let’s see how EOSE can keep pace.

Eos Energy in Brief

Eos was founded in 2008 and is headquartered in Edison, New Jersey. The company employs 787 people. It’s been publicly traded since its 2020 IPO.

In January, the company unveiled their latest innovation—a breakthrough in battery energy storage—which they named Indensity:

Indensity™, a next-generation architecture built with Spatial Intelligence that targets up to 1 GWh per acre while delivering industry-leading flexibility and safety.

The key characteristics of the new offering are as follows:

  • Next-level Density: Redefining expectations of battery storage capacity with a compact, stackable, modular design that targets 1 GWh per acre—roughly 4 times that of most other technologies.
  • Breakthrough Scale: Self-contained, weather-ready Indensity Core units with plug-and-play electrical and communications simplify installation—even in previously unusable spaces.
  • Extraordinary Flexibility: Supports 4-16+ hour durations, handles complex cycling with millisecond-fast response, and adapts to demanding applications like AI-driven data centers.
  • Exceptional Safety: Non-flammable zinc chemistry, recyclable components, and advanced cybersecurity controls make Indensity safe enough for mission-critical sites. Limitless Potential: Deploy near data centers, military bases, and urban infrastructure—removing constraints and unlocking progress.

Latest Earnings

The Q4 results weren’t just a miss, but a really bad one. Analysts had projected revenue of $93.8 million, but the company fell short by about 38% of that estimate.

The stock price plunged by roughly half, sliding from $11.3 before earnings to $5.7 within two days after the results were released.

The CEO took full responsibility around the missed guidance:

..execution is what counts. And when you look at our quarter and our year, yes, we set records. Our volume was up. Our margins improved sequentially quarter-over-quarter and year-over-year. We had a great quarter as far as orders being booked,.. But the bottom line is we missed our guidance, and that falls on me as the CEO of the company.

And promised to execute better:

…we are going to work to make sure that, that doesn't happen again in the future.

Outlook

A broader perspective on the outlook was presented as follows:

...and the 2026 outlook, which fell short of the $471 million estimate.

The Street is taking management’s projections pretty cautiously after a wide miss last quarter, with revenue forecasted to be just over $300 million:

I think it’s worth noting that during the earnings conference call, when asked about projected production capacity by the end of 2026, management responded as follows:

..the target for the year is 4 gigawatt hours of nameplate capacity coming out of 2026. That matches with where we see our backlog. And then from there, we'll be able to add capacity as required.

This suggests that, despite entering 2026 with a $701.5 million (2.8 GWh) backlog, Eos will still need substantial new orders during the year to meet the 2027 analyst revenue expectation of roughly $600 million, which corresponds to around 2 GWh. Furthermore, given the industry's long development cycles from project planning to grid connection, having multiple years of committed backlog is essential, and analysts will focus heavily on that figure when results are announced.

My calculations indicate that Eos’s planned capacity could support around $1 billion in revenue in 2027.

Preliminary results were announced on April 9

The company’s preliminary results weren’t anything remarkable, with Q1 sales of $56-$57 million coming in about the same as Q4. Even so, it sparked a relief rally of nearly 30% in EOSE stock, likely helped by its high short ratio. For me, the bigger news was hearing that Line 2 is making great progress, with initial production set for the end of the second quarter, paving the way toward meeting the 2026 targets.

The preliminary result was neither a beat nor a miss from the company’s own estimates, as the company does not provide quarterly forecasts. According to SA data, seven Street Analysts cover the company, with four of them having lowered their estimates in the past three months:

Given the latest quarter’s brutal miss, the high level of skepticism, and the stock’s significant short interest, I think it’s fair to expect quite a bit of volatility after the upcoming earnings announcement.

Valuation

Putting any accurate valuation tag on EOSE is difficult, in my view. I haven’t seen the company provide guidance on when breakeven could be reached. Street estimates vary widely, but since they’re the only reliable source we have, we need to use them. The more optimistic scenarios point to profitability already in 2028, with revenue around $1.12 billion, which would definitely be a very positive outcome. Whether that is realistic is hard to judge — demand is clearly there, as highlighted earlier, but this is a new technology being ramped up, and the early stages have been quite bumpy.

From a relative valuation perspective, the problem is the lack of pure‑play utility‑scale storage peers. Fluence (FLNC) is the closest comparison, but it operates in the conventional Li‑ion space and is largely viewed as a system integrator and project-focused provider, whereas Eos is building its own unique battery chemistry from the ground up. Despite double‑digit growth, FLNC also continues to struggle meaningfully in terms of profitability.

If we try to extract comparable data from Tesla, it doesn’t help much either. They don’t provide detailed financials for the Energy segment — just revenue and GWh metrics — and the main takeaway from their reports is that the segment operates with gross margins in the low 20s, and they don't see any growth slowdown.

A further complication in the valuation process is the uncertainty around EOSE’s potential dilution. The company has multiple classes of warrants and preferred shares with different conversion terms. The latest proxy filing for the annual shareholder meeting shows that Cerberus affiliate Denami Equity Holdings would hold 31.98% of the company—about 159 million shares—if all their warrants and preferred stock were converted. That would bring the total common shares to around 500 million after the dilution.

Given the board’s proposal to shareholders meeting to authorize an additional 200 million shares on top of the currently authorized 600 million — raising the authorized issuable share count to 800 million — and the high likelihood that the company will need further funding, I think it is more conservative to run valuation scenarios using 600 million shares. At a $7 share price, that implies a current equity valuation of about $4.2 billion.

If EOSE manages to reach the estimated 2028 revenue target of $1.12 billion and continues to grow at around 30%+, then $2 billion in sales by 2030 seems achievable. On that basis, a $4.2 billion valuation suggests a P/S of about 2.1x, which isn’t demanding considering EOSE could be seen as the sole player in its class with patented, globally scalable technology and apparently no demand concerns on the horizon.

But that’s a big "if" — the company still needs to prove that its narrative works in real‑world conditions and at scale. If everything goes according to the best‑case scenario, and given that the technology is globally scalable, reaching even $10 billion in sales sometime in the 2030s is not unrealistic in my view. Tesla’s Energy segment is already guiding toward $16–18 billion and does not expect growth to slow.

So, to sum up, I think investors need to treat EOSE as a genuinely high‑risk bet — in the very literal sense — and size their allocation accordingly. But if it works, the upside could be substantial. I rate EOSE as a buy.

Risks

I can name dozens of notable risks — a new technology ramp‑up naturally carries plenty — but at this stage, I think they can largely be summarized as execution risks. I haven’t found anything fundamentally wrong with their core narrative; all the building blocks seem to be in place for success. As is often the case with new technologies, anything can happen during the ramp-up phase, and recent misses and setbacks in scaling have already proven that.

Another meaningful risk from a shareholder standpoint is dilution. Since we don’t know how far away the breakeven point is — and therefore when the company might stop burning cash — we also don’t know how much additional funding will be required to reach at least a roughly cash‑neutral position.

Takeaway

The demand is there. A solution that is a strong match for that demand is there. The funding is in place, and the going‑concern warning has been removed. On top of that, the administration is providing support through both DOE loans and warrants convertible into equity, reflecting the strong security profile.

If we set aside the fact that execution has been bumpy so far — and communication arguably even more so — we can somewhat mitigate those concerns by remembering that this is a new technology being built from scratch. Setbacks at this stage are more or less expected. The leadership team has also been strengthened recently, with key hires bringing experience in scaling operations. Many of the senior leaders have strong GE backgrounds.

I believe this is a classic high‑risk, high‑reward investment, and at current levels it looks attractive. That said, given the elevated risk profile, the position size should remain modest.

data.title: Firefly Aerospace Has Room To Run
data.description:
Last fall, I initiated coverage on Firefly Aerospace Inc. (FLY) with a buy rating and stated that the stock was a bet on the new space race. Back then, I found the NASA partnerships to be encouraging and the growing backlog to be a promising sign for future growth. The stock is up over 60% since the publication of that article. Recently, there has undoubted been a build up of optimism in the space-tech sector in general as the Artemis II mission was successful and Elon Musk's SpaceX (SPACE) prepares to go public. At this point, there is really no question that the new space race is happening and so today I thought I would provide an update for readers at this important time.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Firefly Aerospace had a strong Q4. Furthermore, their financial position looks quite solid now and their forward looking revenue guidance shows that an acceleration in business momentum is likely. While there are some risks that we will talk about, overall recent developments show that the company is capturing the opportunity at hand nicely. Their 'Stairway to Seven' mission also helped to restore confidence and so the forward P/S contraction since my October article seems well overdone. As a result, I have decided to remain bullish on Firefly Aerospace stock.

A Full Stack Space Company

To start off today's analysis, it is important to highlight the fact that Firefly Aerospace has really become a full stack space infrastructure company. There isn't a shortage of competition for contracts during this new space race but the company seems to be one of the few that offers a full spectrum of solutions. As you can see above, they have the capability to launch and they also have spacecrafts.

Furthermore, through their acquisition of SciTec, they are also very much a player on the software side of things as well. There are always user friendliness and compatibility advantages when you offer the full stack and so I view that as a competitive advantage that Firefly Aerospace has moving forward. Before looking at some important recent developments for the company, let's turn to a look at some of their financials.

Growth Is Robust

A look at the numbers shows that Firefly Aerospace is seeing strong business momentum. For 2025 Q4, the company generated revenue of $57.7 million which represents a growth rate of 541% YoY. This is a massive acceleration of growth from the previous quarter and they also beat analyst expectations by $5.26 million. We'll talk more about their backlog in just a second but given these top line numbers it seems that they are effective in monetizing the opportunity that's present right now.

Moving forward, Firefly Aerospace expects to see between $420 million and $450 million worth of revenues for full year 2026. This implies a 172% growth rate and would be an acceleration from the 163% 2025 increase that's shown above. In their earnings call, it is mentioned that this strength will be powered by Alpha launches, execution on Blue Ghost missions, Eclipse and Elytra, as well as government software. Overall the outlook for business activity is solid and shows that their momentum will accelerate.

Backlog Seems Healthy

As a signal that demand for their space solutions remains solid, their total backlog increased by 23% YoY. The SciTech acquisition did add around $170 million to their backlog balance but nonetheless, there was clearly organic growth.

While there have been significant progress in many areas of their business, a highlight would probably be Blue Ghost. Their Mission 2 is set to launch either in late 2026 Q4 or early 2027 Q1 and so a significant amount of backlog will be monetized. In addition, back in 2025 they won the Blue Ghost Mission 4 contract from NASA. Systems Requirement Review has been completed and so over the next few years it seems that Firefly Aerospace will continue to translate that extensive backlog into financial growth.

Financial Position

In Q4, Firefly Aerospace's operating loss widened but their net loss narrowed. Nonetheless, at this point, a look at their cash flows would probably be the most valuable. As shown above, the company's operations burned through $67.26 million in the fourth quarter while FCFs were at -$79.33 million. It is clear that with the company focusing on R&D and growth, positive cash flows are unlikely in the near term.

While that is the case, Firefly Aerospace's cash and liquidity position shows that new capital raises will be unnecessary for the foreseeable future. At the end of 2025, the company had cash and cash equivalents of $792.966 million while having another $100.008 million of short-term investments. Unless cash burn picks up sharply, this cash pile should provide investors with some confidence that dilution is not a major risk in the next few quarters.

Lastly, as you can see above, their current ratio is at a very comfortable level of 4.51 and so liquidity is definitely not a problem at this point. As I whole, I view Firefly Aerospace's financial position as favorable and that should be a good foundation for this business to continue growing.

Recent Events

Back in my previous article, it was highlighted that their Alpha Flight 7 rocket had experienced a considerable setback with the booster stage having exploded. On March 12th, it was reported that their 'Stairway to Seven' mission was successful. As a part of this mission, they validated key Alpha Block II upgrades as well and that provides a big boost to their Alpha Flight 8 ambitions. Overall, the success of this launch was crucial to restore investor confidence and they delivered.

In late March, Firefly Aerospace and Lockheed Martin (LMT) performed an exercise known as Victus Diem and the objective was to see how fast satellites can be launched in an emergency type situation. As Seeking Alpha's Rob Williams writes

As part of the drill, the companies completed payload processing in less than 12 hours, a step that typically takes significantly longer in traditional launch schedules. The effort was designed to demonstrate how quickly a satellite payload could be readied for launch during a crisis scenario.

Lockheed Martin is obviously one of the largest military contractors out there but Firefly Aerospace's participation in this exercise shows that their innovation and importance to national security is now very significant. As the company continues to pursue the defense market, milestones like these could be key.

Last but not least, earlier this month Firefly Aerospace announced a collab with Nvidia (NVDA) for their moon imaging project. Basically, Jetson modules were put in telescopes that will in turn be integrated into the company's Elytra orbital vehicle. As CEO Jason Kim states

Ocula will be powered by the world’s leading edge AI processor. This capability allows us to layer on our SciTec AI software as the ‘brains’ that give customers real-time, data-driven insights from the Moon.

Note that Ocula will be part of the Blue Ghost Mission 2 that was mentioned earlier. In my view, this collab with Nvidia is likely to increase the significance of this Moon imaging project significantly since using top edge AI chips will help to generate more useful insights for customers. Over time, this strong value proposition will of course be beneficial to their business.

Bull Thesis Risks

In my recent article on Intuitive Machines (LUNR), I mentioned two key stock performance risks that apply to Firefly Aerospace as well.

Firstly, while the prospects of the company are getting increasingly strong, there is little doubt that the recent rally in space stocks has at least in part been driven by the successful Artemis II mission. Note that the timeline for Artemis is ambitious moving forward and the program has a long history of delays. If significant setbacks are reported for this major NASA program down the road, the space sector as a whole could see weak sentiment.

The second risk would be the SpaceX IPO. While the buzz regarding this Musk-led company going public may have been another driver for strong performance in space stocks recently, post-IPO there is a risk that investors may rotate their capital from smaller space plays into the undisputed leader. If this happens, Firefly Aerospace stock could see some short term weakness.

Valuation Provides Opportunity

In my previous article, Firefly Aerospace's forward P/S ratio was at a reading of 28.59. Despite the recent rebound, the current 15.81 level in the multiple shows that there has been significant contraction since last fall. I believe this provides investors with a potentially even more attractive risk/reward setup.

Today, many encouraging signs were shown. For Q4, the financials looked robust and their financial position also seems strong enough for investors to not have to worry about further dilution in the near term. Revenue guidance for 2026 shows that business momentum is accelerating and the recent events discussed just earlier show that the company's growth story remains on track for the most part. There are some risks in the form of potential Artemis delays weighing on sentiment or the SpaceX IPO causing capital rotation but as a whole, long term prospects seem stronger than ever.

With that being the case, I would say that the forward P/S contraction since last fall is hard to justify. Their recent 'Stairway to Seven' success has restored confidence in the company's capabilities and so if the valuation was worth it back in October, I would say that Firefly Aerospace has become very attractive for risk-tolerant investors.

Looking Forward To Q1

As we head into their 2026 Q1 earnings, there are some things to keep in mind. While the space race has taken off recently, it is important to remember that this is unlikely to significantly boost near term revenues. Firefly Aerospace can't simply rush their missions to increase revenue recognition and so I believe a major full year revenue guidance raise is off the table for the most part. However, there are some things that investors should look for.

Firstly, a key metric worth monitoring would be the backlog numbers. If there is an increase in demand from the space race taking off, this will likely be where it first shows up. Look for an increase in contracts as a signal that customers are choosing their full stack solutions over their competitors.

Secondly, keeping an eye on the cash burn would be wise. While their cash pile is fair, the company seems to be on a trend of increasing cash burn. If their growth requires an extensive amount of capital, investors should be prepared for dilution. Overall, looking forward to Q1, I encourage investors to look past their results for the quarter and near term guidance as the most important clues will be their backlog and cash burn.

Final Thoughts

Firefly Aerospace has seen monstrous gains of over 60% since my previous article but I believe taking profits now would be a mistake. In my view the stock has room to run and the current fundamental setup is favorable for continued outperformance. With the financials looking good, outlook getting increasingly bright, and the valuation not being at a historically elevated position, investors that don't mind relatively speculative stocks should be justifiably tempted. Therefore, I have decided to maintain my buy rating on Firefly Aerospace as this new space race is only just beginning.

data.title: Zions Bancorporation: NII Upswing, More Upside In 2026
data.description:
Zions Bancorporation (ZION) submitted better-than-expected earnings for its first-quarter on April 20, 2026 which were supported by higher net interest income and NI margins, a favorable deposit mix on the balance sheet, as well as strong asset performance. The regional bank, which benefits from strong loan demand in a well-performing U.S. economy, especially in the commercial and industrial lending businesses, has seen double-digit earnings growth in the last quarter, which marked a continuation of a strong overall loan expansion record all throughout 2025.

The Federal Reserve has been more cautious lately to move federal fund rates lower (which benefits banks), and the bank guided for a moderate increase in its net interest income as a result. While the bank's shares are not a total bargain based off of book value, they offer enough value to investors, in my opinion, to justify a continued buy rating.

Previous rating

When I reviewed Zions Bancorporation the last time in January 2026, I rated the regional lender a 'Buy' -- Strong Investment Setup -- mainly because of its robust underlying growth of its commercial loan portfolio and attractive NII prospects. Recently, interest rate expectations have shifted in favor of a higher-for-longer rate environment, with the Federal Reserve delaying rate cuts in March. Higher inflation as well as strong credit expansion are two reasons here, in my opinion, to remain invested in 2026.

Zions Bancorporation beat Q1’26 estimates

The regional banking platform reported better-than-expected earnings for its top and its bottom line on Monday in what has so far been a strong earnings reporting season for banks in general: Zions Bancorporation published non-GAAP earnings of $1.54 per-share which surpassed the consensus estimate by $0.11 per-share. It was the fourth consecutive quarter in which the regional lender beat EPS expectations. The revenue figure came in at $860M and beat the average prediction by $4.6M.

Zions Bancorporation benefited in the first fiscal quarter from a massive upswing in its net earnings, which was supported by net interest margin gains, strong performance in its core loan business as well as higher fee-related income. The bank reported total earnings of $232M, showing a massive 37% year-over-year jump, with its customer-related non-interest income (+10% Y/Y) being largely responsible for this growth. This 10% fee-related growth came from commercial account fee, treasury management, and loan fees related to residential mortgage sales.

A key source of growth for Zions Bancorporation was the expansion of its loan portfolio, especially the part that handles commercial and specialized loans to industrial sectors. In Q1’26, the regional banking platform had a total of $61.1B in average total loans on its balance sheet, showing 2.5% year-over-year growth.

Specifically, this growth was fueled by 1) Robust lending activity in the core Commercial and Industrial (C&I) loan segment, and 2) Modest growth in commercial real estate loans, and 3) Higher loan activity in the HELOC (home equity lines of credit) category. By focusing on shifting away from lower-yielding securities and into higher-yielding commercial loans, the bank managed to grow its net interest margin compared to the year-earlier period as well.

Zions Bancorporation’s net interest income in Q1’26 dropped sequentially, due to two fewer days in the quarter and seasonally higher funding costs, but rose 6% year-over-year amid favorable changes in the asset mix and higher benchmark interest rates. Compared to some of its regional banking peers, like PNC Financial Services (PNC), the bank saw more moderate growth in its NII.

While PNC Financial Services saw 14% year-over-year growth in net interest income in Q1’26 (bolstered by its acquisition of FirstBank), Zions Bancorporation achieved its growth more through organic balance sheet optimization and a shift away from high-cost brokered deposits. I just discussed PNC's value proposition here: Not A Great Buy Right Now.

Zions Bancorporation is actively lowering its exposure to lower-yield securities and high-cost deposits (as discussed above) and rotating assets into higher-yielding commercial loans which have been a core driver of the lender's NII growth in Q1'26.

Besides tapping into its loan portfolio to drive net interest income gains, the Zions Bancorporation also generated strong growth for its fee-related revenue streams… which are mainly tied to the bank’s commercial account and wealth management services. In the most recent quarter, Zions Bancorporation published $174M in adjusted fee income (which strips out securities gains), showing 10% year-over-year growth.

ZION: moderate upside left on the table on NII expansion

The regional bank has seen significant valuation pressure in February and in March after the U.S. escalated its conflict with Iran into a new military campaign. However, hopes of a peace settlement have caused a resurgence of investor optimism lately, the stock market bouncing back strongly in March with price gains extending all the way into mid-April. Zions Bancorporation has almost fully recovered its valuation losses sustained during the last market draw-down, yet shares may have more upside potential, especially if the Federal Reserve goes easy on federal fund rate cuts in 2026.

Shares of Zions Bancorporation have a price-to-book ratio of 1.31X which is below the industry group average P/B ratio of 1.42X, but 11% above the bank’s longer term, 3-year average price-to-book ratio of 1.18X. The increase in the P/B ratio in the last 2-3 years is due to the changing interest rate landscape and an unwillingness to lower federal fund rates on the part of the Federal Reserve, despite broadly declining inflation rates since 2024.

Zions Bancorporation has similar lending business models as U.S. Bancorp (USB) and PNC Financial Services, meaning it drives aggressive loan expansion in the commercial and industrial core markets. ZION is supplementing its lending growth with fee streams related to commercial banking activities.

If ZION rerated to the industry group average of 1.42X, which would be in-line with the valuation ratios of regional bank peers, shares of Zions Bancorporation could have a fair value in the neighborhood of $69.80 per-share, using a last reported BVPS of $49.16 (+16% Y/Y). The price target represents approximately 11% upside revaluation potential from Monday's closing price of $63.05.

Risks with ZION

Zions Bancorporations faces two medium term risks: 1) The regional bank’s balance sheet is sensitive to changes in benchmark interest rates meaning a faster pace of Federal Reserve rate cuts could impact Zions Bancorporation’s NII outlook, and 2) A recession could potentially increase loan defaults and negatively affect the firm's pre-provisioning income. These risks, however, are of general nature and affect all financial institutions. What would get me to change my mind about Zions Bancorporation is if the lender failed to grow its net interest income in 2026.

Final thoughts

Zions Bancorporation exceeded expectations for its first fiscal quarter due to an expansion in its loan portfolio, which was driven by new loans in commercial and industrial segments, and higher net interest income as well as an upsurge in fee-related earnings. This reconciles with the earnings reports of other (regional) banks we have seen this earnings season as financial institutions generally benefited from a strong lending environment and robust economic growth backing rising credit demand. I continue to like the regional lender for its growth profile and higher NII in a higher-for-longer rate environment and would recommend Zions Bancorporation for investors that seek exposure to a well-run regional banking franchise.

data.title: The Silver Squeeze: Strong Today, Uncertain Tomorrow
data.description:
I don't know about you, but from an investment perspective, I am not terribly enthusiastic about gold. I do feel differently, however, about silver, even if it doesn't look as visually stunning. This is because, unlike gold, which is used in large quantities solely as a store of value and a means by which market participants speculate, silver is a different animal entirely. I say this because much of what is dug up from the ground is ultimately allocated toward specific end uses. Certainly, there is an investment angle to this. But it is clear that silver is more akin to other metals like copper, aluminum, etc., in that it derives much of its value from demand that falls outside of speculation.

Over the past year, however, the picture has changed some. From 2016 through 2024, the price of silver rose quite modestly, appreciating 64.9%, or 6.5% per annum, as it marched from $17.14 per oz to $28.27 per oz. But then, in 2025, prices exploded higher. Official data has not yet been provided for an average price. But based on third-party sources, I estimated that the average price was around $40.16 per oz. But that's not all. As of this writing, silver stands at $80.93 per oz. That represents a surge of 186.3% from the average that we have seen in 2024. Near term, I wouldn't be surprised to see silver prices increase further. There does seem to be a supply issue here, and demand for certain categories is rising. Having said that, I would argue that, in the long run, there are reasons to believe that the price will moderate some. Because of this, I believe that taking a more neutral stance on the precious metal is appropriate. But I can definitely understand why someone might disagree with me.

Silver’s supply problem

*in Millions of Ounces.

When digging into the data regarding silver, the first thing that I noticed was that there has developed a mismatch between global supply and global demand. In the chart above, you can see what global supply and global demand have been over the last several years dating from 2016 through 2025. You can also see that, in each of the last five years now, demand has exceeded supply by quite a bit. The shortfall last year, for instance, amounted to 10.2% of demand, or approximately 11.4% of supply.

What's really concerning here is that the picture looks even worse when we look at total mine production. Although it has risen now for the last three years, the amount that came from mines last year was lower to the tune of 7.2% than it was back in 2016. The fact of the matter is that the silver industry has faced certain challenges in recent years. According to The Silver Institute, low prices after the mid 2010s discouraged investment from producers in silver. And a lot of what is currently mined is actually done so as a byproduct of other metals, with examples being copper, gold, zinc, and the like. That means that, as production from those other metals changes, we should see volatility when it comes to silver also. And then when you add on top of this certain disruptions, like those associated with the COVID-19 pandemic that negatively affected output from China, Mexico, and Peru, it's not difficult to see why overall mining activity had fallen.

Increasingly, we have seen recycled silver account for a growing share of supply. Back in 2016, recycled silver amounted to approximately 14.8% of global supply. But by 2025, that amount had surged 23.6%, growing to 18.7% of supply. Even though supply is supply all the same, what this means is that, each and every year, the amount of silver that is being injected into the market fresh from the ground is being outpaced by growing demand. That would naturally put upward pressure on prices at some point. But we also have the fact that demand is indeed expanding.

From 2016 through 2025, global demand for silver ended up jumping 15.6%. Now, to be fair, we have actually seen a drop over the last four years amounting to 10.6%. However, certain categories of demand continue to show strength. From 2016 through 2025, for instance, the amount of silver used in photovoltaics that are necessary for solar power skyrocketed 139.8%. That has taken it up from accounting for only 8.2% of global demand to being responsible for consuming 17% of it. There have been other changes as well. But overwhelmingly, photovoltaics and electrical and electronic products more broadly, have been problematic for it.

Those who are bullish about silver will almost certainly point toward the expectation of a continued expansion in the solar industry. Back in 2010, global photovoltaic energy capacity amounted to just 40GW. That number ballooned to 1TW by 2022 before doubling by 2024. Estimates vary, but as we continue to shift toward a carbon-neutral world, the amount of capacity needed for solar power will expand to between 18TW and 63TW by the year 2050. One estimate said that, by that time, solar power will account for 50% of global electricity generation. That means that we would have to see capacity of about 12.5TW, though it did also leave open the door for additional growth beyond this base case scenario.

Although the data is a bit outdated, we can see in the chart below projections for solar demand from 2010 through 2027. This is specifically the share of cumulative power capacity that was projected when this analysis was conducted back in 2023. By 2027, it was estimated that about 22.2% of power capacity globally would be accounted for using solar energy. This is thanks to significant investments being made by not only the US, but by China, India, and the European Union.

We also have another catalyst that is almost certainly responsible for a lot of the move higher that silver prices have endured. This would be concern about the global economic environment. The US has become increasingly unstable from a geopolitical standpoint. The most recent government spending bills, and I mean recent as in over the last couple of administrations, have pointed toward a growth in debt related spending. So when you add together economic uncertainty with geopolitical risk, there is naturally going to be a flight to safety. Gold has historically been that main source outside of the US dollar. But with gold prices having skyrocketed over the last couple of years now, it makes sense that investors and speculators alike would buy more heavily into silver than they have in the past. Although silver consumption as a means of physical investment is lower than it was during the COVID-19 pandemic, it is still 13.2% above what it was for the average of the four years ending in 2019.

In February of this year, The Silver Institute provided their own assessment of what is going on right now and what will occur throughout 2026. They unfortunately do project that this year will be the 6th consecutive annual market deficit that silver will experience. In addition to citing geopolitical and U.S. policy uncertainty, they also mentioned that worries over the independence of the Federal Reserve have been a driver of increased demand for silver. The expectation is that while demand this year will be pretty much flat compared to last year, that will only be because of steep declines in certain categories like in jewelry and silverware. There is expected to be a 2% drop in demand when it comes to industrial fabrication. And a lot of that can be chalked up to near term pain for the photovoltaic sector as the rise in global solar installations will be dealt with through thrifting and substitution away from silver that will see overall demand for silver decline even as solar investment ramps up. Supply, meanwhile, is expected to rise by about 1.5%. This is thanks in part to the thought that silver mine production will grow by 1% thanks to recently commissioned projects such as those in Mexico and China.

In the near term, this could certainly push prices lower. But it is my own personal belief that worsening economic conditions in the US as we barrel toward a recession, as well as heightened political uncertainty, will likely offset this downward pressure. Long term, however, we will see certain challenges facing the space. This is thanks in large part to increased substitution that will make silver less prevalent as a component in photovoltaics.

Copper is one interesting alternative. It is cheap, and it is estimated to be around 94% as conductive as silver is. This is not to say that it is perfect. There are certain limitations, such as the risk of corrosion and degradation. But that doesn't mean that investments aren't being made in this regard. In January of this year, for instance, Chinese module manufacturer Longi revealed that it will begin mass production of base metal photovoltaic cells in the second quarter of this year, essentially transitioning toward copper based metallization because of rising silver prices and cost pressures. It's not the only company moving that way. In fact, according to the aforementioned source, it's believed that there will be a 7% drop in demand for silver specifically when it comes to photovoltaic modules this year.

There are other examples of a shift away from silver. New designs of photovoltaic cells that utilize more efficient production methods have allowed a continuous decline in silver grams for each watt. And investments in silver free architectures, though still early stage, offer promise. One example of this can be seen by looking at heterojunction solar cell screens that are printed using silver copper on one side and silver paste on another in a manner that captures additional efficiencies while reducing the reliance on silver as a standalone metal. With costs as high as they are right now, it is likely that investments are going to increase in areas such as this.

Takeaway

To be honest with you, the only reason I am not bearish on silver this moment is because of the political and economic climate that we find ourselves in. There are certain structural issues as I have detailed already that are problematic for silver demand moving forward. We could see some weakness in certain areas this year, such as those involving photovoltaic cells. But with demand still outpacing supply this year and worries about geopolitical and economic conditions, I think that the near term picture will be supportive of these elevated prices. That's why, for now, I think that a neutral stance on silver is justified. But in the event that we start to see the economic and political climate stabilize, I would turn bearish on it very quickly.

data.title: Lord Abbett Total Return Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • The first quarter was defined by a balance between economic resilience and rising geopolitical and macro uncertainty. Market performance faced headwinds from weakness in Technology/Software (driven by AI disruption concerns) and escalating geopolitical tensions, though supportive macroeconomic conditions helped offset some of the pressure. Labor market signals were mixed and inflation remained above target, but expectations for earnings growth, fiscal stimulus, and eventual tariff stabilization helped anchor the outlook. Late in the quarter, geopolitical risks intensified as tensions in the Middle East disrupted energy markets and pushed oil prices higher, delaying expectations for rate cuts and introducing near-term inflation risks. Despite this backdrop, the underlying economy remained constructive, supported by solid consumer spending, continued earnings momentum, and expectations for longer-term policy support.
  • Over the quarter, investment grade corporate 2 credit spreads widened by 11 basis points (bps), commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) 3 spreads tightened by 1 bp, and asset-backed securities (ABS) 4 spreads were flat. The 2-Year U.S. Treasury yield increased from 3.48% to 3.79% and the 10-Year U.S. Treasury yield moved higher from 4.17% to 4.32% 1 .

Portfolio Review

  • The Fund returned -0.28%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested, for the quarter ended March 31 st , 2026. The Fund’s benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index*, returned -0.05% during the same period.
  • The portfolio’s rates positioning was the primary detractor from relative performance over the quarter. In particular, the portfolio’s modestly longer duration detracted from relative performance as rates moved higher over the quarter.
  • Security selection within investment grade corporate bonds also detracted from relative performance, particularly within the Communications and Technology sectors. Over the quarter, we decreased the portfolio’s allocation to investment grade corporate bonds, particularly in sectors more vulnerable to AI disruptions.
  • An allocation to non-agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) was the primary contributor to relative performance. Over the quarter, we increased the portfolio’s exposure to non-agency MBS, primarily through AAA-rated non-qualified mortgage securities where we see attractive relative value.
  • Security selection within agency mortgage-backed securities also contributed to relative performance. We increased exposure to agency MBS during the period given the sector’s high quality and liquidity amid the market volatility.

Mortgage-backed securities (MBS) are variations of asset-backed securities that are formed by pooling together mortgages exclusively.

Commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) are fixed-income investment products that are backed by mortgages on commercial properties rather than residential real estate.

A collateralized loan obligation (CLO) is a single security backed by a pool of debt. Collateralized loan obligations (CLO) are often backed by corporate loans with low credit ratings or loans taken out by private equity firms to conduct leveraged buyouts.

data.title: This Market Rally Has An Elephant In The Room: The AI CapEx Cycle
data.description:
Markets have steadily rallied for the past week, following news that the situation in Iran may be getting to a swift conclusion. Personally, I am somewhat surprised that Tech names have rallied more than the broader market, resulting in the NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX) outperforming the S&P500 (SP500).

Markets seem to have forgotten what used to be their main worry just a couple of months ago: AI CapEx. However, nothing really has changed in that regard. If anything, it would be logical to expect indices like the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) to outperform on news that oil and energy prices may return to pre-war levels without affecting consumption or risking a recession.

Today, I make the case that this relief rally can only last as long as the AI CapEx narrative doesn't collapse. Once again, while markets were worried about noise from Iran, the future of America’s economy (and equity markets) was tied to the AI story.

Just how AI-dependent is the market narrative?

Readers may be surprised to learn that purely in macroeconomic terms, AI CapEx is probably not fully responsible for the US economy's growth story. This is the conclusion of a recent JP Morgan report that argues how there is a relatively limited impact of AI investment on GDP in strict terms.

However, when it comes to equity markets, the story is different. In late 2025, Morgan Stanley noted that AI-related companies accounted for 75% of the S&P 500's gains, 80% of its profit growth, and 90% of its CapEx growth.

Overall, it is clear in my opinion that what is currently driving returns is the expectation (someone would say “hope”) of high ROI from AI investments. This narrative, in my view, will once again take over once the Iran situation is fully solved.

Not an AI CapEx Cycle, but an AI CapEx Bet

In terms of weight on US GDP, AI CapEx has been on a steady growth since AI became a topic and it is on track to reach 2% of GDP in 2026 (see chart below).

This is why I do not like to talk about an AI CapEx “cycle”. Rather, I prefer to think about it as a great bet. In my view, AI CapEx will either keep growing to generate more compute power and “feed” AI applications or deflate completely, if AI turns out to be a bubble.

This distinction is important because thinking about AI CapEx as a cycle could imply having to time said cycle, similarly to what investors may want to do if they trade in commodity markets. AI is not a cycle in my view, but rather an accelerating force that may end up reshaping our economy.

There are three types of AI companies: Enablers, Creators, Adopters

To frame the AI race, I present today the idea that there are three types of AI companies:

  1. Companies that focus on AI infrastructure (e.g., energy, microchips, datacenters). They are not exposed to AI CapEx themselves, but reap the benefits of AI CapEx by providing what’s needed to research AI models. Examples are NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) or Micron Technology, Inc. (MU).

  2. Companies that focus on building new, ever more powerful AI models. These are the companies that spend most, if not all, AI CapEx. Examples are OpenAI (OPENAI), Anthropic (ANTHRO) or even integrated businesses like Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) or Meta Platforms, Inc. (META).

  3. Companies that adopt AI in the “application layer”, i.e. those that leverage third parties’ AI in their businesses or with their customers but have no exposure to AI CapEx in any way. The most prominent example of a winner here is Palantir Technologies, Inc. (PLTR), but a lot of other companies ranging from Adobe Inc. (ADBE) to Duolingo, Inc. (DUOL) are adopting AI, with varying success.

AI Enablers: the current winners of the AI race

AI enablers are easy to understand: they provide the very tools that are needed to develop, run and enhance LLMs (Large Language Models). A selection of 14 tickers in this space (see below) has returned more than 6X the original investment in the last five years, massively outperforming the broader market.

Whether or not these returns (and the rich valuations of the companies behind them) can be sustained, will very much depend on the direction of AI and whether CapEx from “creators” can be sustained.

AI Creators: winning (for now), despite being stuck with CapEx

AI creators are probably the hottest topic at the moment. These are companies that are also often called “AI Hyperscalers”. Personally, I do not necessarily like this definition as I think any company adopting and accelerating AI could be defined as a Hyperscaler, even if they have no plan for CapEx. Regardless, CapEx from these companies is expected to grow significantly in the future.

The two most notable companies in this space are OpenAI and Anthropic, which are both not publicly listed at the time of writing but reportedly trade at multi-billion dollar valuations in the secondary market. A portfolio made of 7 publicly listed companies in this space has also performed surprisingly well, ahead of the broader market (see chart below).

In my view, these returns have taken place as the market took a “rain check” on AI ROI. These are, of course, high-growth and highly profitable companies. But they still have relatively little to show for their investment in AI CapEx.

While companies like META have successfully adopted AI to improve the efficacy of their Advertising business, overall returns remain unclear. Google, for example, which is leading the AI race in many ways, still generated more than 50% of revenue from Search Ads at the time of writing. Overall, the business model of these companies has not changed yet - they still sell cloud solutions and software as they did before AI (albeit at a faster pace, in most cases).

AI Adopters: this is where the next Alpha will come from

AI adopters are companies that have almost no exposure to AI CapEx, yet are visibly leveraging AI solutions in their businesses. This is the most complex category to discuss, because AI adoption currently varies greatly among different companies and sectors, and so do the results of said adoption.

Even the very definition of “adopting AI” is up to debate, with numerous studies suggesting only about ~20% or less of US companies are doing so.

It is no surprise then that a basket of “AI Adopters” stocks across different sectors has had mixed results in the past five years (see table below).
