Personally, I believe most future “Alpha” will be generated from this third set of companies (note: not necessarily my selection of tickers, but companies in this category).

While I am also bullish on Nvidia, Microsoft and other “Hyperscalers”, I think asymmetric market returns will predominantly come from companies that the market has not yet fully priced as AI winners.

How to invest in an AI world

As I argued multiple times in the past, I believe AI will not disrupt all businesses but rather produce winners and losers. Within the framework I just outlined, I think there are two ways to invest in today’s AI age:

  1. Trying to select AI winners; an inherently difficult task that can however deliver incredible results.

  2. Playing AI momentum and sentiment by investing in Tech-heavy indices, potentially with some leverage.

For 2026, I am personally favoring a balanced approach between the two options. Most of my work on Seeking Alpha consists of covering those companies that I consider have a good chance at becoming AI winners.

AI adoption: a tale of extremes, narrated by Mr. Market

To better understand just how early we are in the AI adoption phase, consider three examples.

The first is Duolingo, a company I previously covered on this platform. The market currently prices DUOL as if their business is going to be completely disrupted by AI. Yet, the company has only grown both top and bottom line in the past few years and may be leveraging AI to offer tailored educational solutions to users, ultimately helping growth. Whether or not Duolingo’s approach will work may become clear only in the next months (or, potentially, years).

At the other end of the spectrum we have Palantir, the single stock I discussed the most here on Seeking Alpha. This is by any financial metric an AI winner, currently growing at a Rule of 40 of 127%. Palantir’s “secret sauce” is that it treats LLMs as commodities, incorporating them in their model-agnostic software. Palantir’s moat is built by locking-in clients with their own software, rather than by selling an ever-more-powerful version of an existing LLM (which seems to be the playbook of Anthropic at the moment).

If one were to analyze these two companies without looking at their stock price, both could be considered AI early adopters and “winners”. Yet, the market is pricing one an AI loser and one as an AI winner.

The Apple case: what if this is the next big AI play?

Another interesting case of a company that may, surprisingly, end up being an AI disruptor is Apple Inc. (AAPL). Apple has come under criticism in the past for not having actively participated in the AI race. Their AI CapEx is almost nonexistent, as the chart below shows.

Apple has adopted a completely different strategy, which somewhat resonates with that of Palantir: a model-agnostic AI approach. The company has recently partnered with multiple firms to deploy their AI models within Apple’s own ecosystem.

This is not an article about Apple, so I won’t deep dive into how the company is integrating third-party AI in their solutions. What I will say is that if their integration is successful, Apple could end up having a competitive advantage against other former peers in the form of higher margins and no exposure to AI CapEx. Given Apple’s stock sluggish performance YTD, it seems to me that the market has not yet priced in this possibility.

Final macro considerations on the AI CapEx cycle

Beyond single stocks, one of the ultimate objectives of the AI CapEx cycle is to develop AI models so powerful that they can eventually reach human-like intelligence. This, I think, would have profound impacts on the economy.

The rapid evolution of AI models (see chart below) suggests we could potentially see AGI (Artificial General Intelligence) materialize in the next months or years.

If this happens, I believe the primary macroeconomic impact will be deflation, driven predominantly by a massive collapse in labor costs as AI and robotics begin replacing human workers at scale.

To counter this unprecedented employment shock and the resulting deflationary wave, policymakers will be forced to respond with aggressive monetary easing. I expect central banks to go beyond traditional rate cuts, potentially introducing monetary-funded UBI (Universal Basic Income) to sustain consumption in what will likely become a deeply K-shaped economy.

In this environment of boundless liquidity and currency debasement, asset owners stand to benefit immensely. As the market is inherently forward-looking, I believe equities may start pricing in such a scenario well before total disruption hits the economy.

This macro perspective, which I covered in more details in my 2026 Market Outlook piece, is why I remain secularly bullish on US equities. It is also the reason why I am recommending a balanced approach between selecting single stocks and staying long in the market long term.

Risks to my thesis

I think the whole market narrative is once again shifting from geopolitics to AI CapEx. The main risk in this regard is that AI ends up being a bubble and AI CapEx deflates. If AI doesn’t prove to deliver top and bottom line results for companies, my idea that Alpha may be generated by CapEx-light AI companies would also ultimately prove wrong.

Another layer of risk regards timing. It may take long for AI to develop to a point that it can be leveraged at scale and have a visible impact on the economy. AGI may be not months but years, or potentially decades, away.

Finally, geopolitics may continue playing a role. Not only the Iran war may re-escalate, but the transition from a society where human labor is central to one where AI is at the center may prove troublesome. In this regard, it is worth noting that AI-driven job disruptions could weaken consumer sentiment and bring the US (and the world) to a recession. Whether policymakers and the voting public could come up with prompt solutions and a plan for a transition to any form of UBI remains to be seen and it is a big bet in itself.

data.title: Flexsteel: Not The Most Compelling Investment, But Not Averse To It Either
data.description:
Introduction

Flexsteel Industries, Inc. (FLXS) which has a 133-year old history in the field of residential furniture and furnishing, currently sells its products in over 2700 retail stores across the country (it is incidentally also amongst the top 10 furniture manufacturers in the US, although 50% of its manufacturing and sourcing comes from abroad), as well as another dozen independent e-tailers. FLXS, whose upholstered furniture is typically used in a living area of a standard home (82% of sales), has the distinction of featuring a UNIQUE drop-in seat spring, which is also the inspiration behind the company’s name.

Nonetheless, while we are only a little over four-and-a-half months into the new year, the stock of Flexsteel appears to be enjoying its time in the sun, having generated returns of almost 18%. This is a solid 16x more than other consumer discretionary stocks (on average), and 1.4x more than FLXS’s counterparts from the Russell 2000. The image below also implies that FLXS’s good fortune this year really kickstarted from February (even if it has given up some of those early gains), and the catalyst behind that spurt was Flexsteel’s Q2-26 earnings event (the company follows a June-ending fiscal).

Now, yet again, an earnings catalyst is underway with FLXS having reported its Q3 earnings yesterday, post market hours. For now, the market appears to be giving the thumbs up with a sub 2% post market reaction; however, this could well change, as at the time of writing, as FLXS was yet to carry out its earnings call (which is scheduled to take place before the market opens on the 21st of April)

So, what can one initially infer about FLXS’s prospects from the recent earnings release, and is the stock worth pursuing at this juncture?

Q3-26 Earnings- Key Talking Points

For starters, it wouldn’t be unfair to term the topline performance in Q3 as quite disappointing. After delivering decent enough net sales growth of 9% YoY in Q2-26, the runrate slowed in a big way to just around 1% YoY growth in Q3-26, marking FLXS’s worst topline performance in 10 quarters!

It wasn’t just the slowing down of growth that was a concern, FLXS’s reported revenue of $115.13M also fell short of street estimates by over -1%. This would seem troubling to a fair few because history has shown us that this is a business that has been accustomed to delivering a topline beat of 3% per quarter on average, and to not manage a beat of any sort will be seen as quite a surprise.

FLXS management noted that Q2 which turned out to be a “choppy quarter” was initially scarred by severe weather conditions, with demand softening as the quarter progressed. Investors would do well not to expect a reversal of this trend any time soon, given the conflict in the Middle East, which has served as yet another nail in the coffin. Also consider that the latest Consumer Sentiment Index, for the US, which can be seen as a proxy for discretionary purchase appetite in the US, dropped to historic lows of just 47.6 (down by 9% YoY and 11% MoM) in April, with buying conditions for household durables in particularly taking another beating. This doesn’t bode well for FLXS’s made-to-order soft seating products that have already been expressing strong volume loss for multiple quarters. Even newer categories such as their health & wellness portfolio, which was previously highlighted as a beacon of “steady progress” are now only seeing “more moderate growth levels than in prior quarters”.

All in all, FLXS’s outlook for the near future doesn’t look bright with the topline now expected to contract by -7% YoY and -4% YoY respectively (consensus expectations), although it’s worth noting that FLXS management still expects a flattish Q4 rather than a decline. Note that consensus has already been trimming the June quarter sales figure by anything between -3% to -10% over the past 1-3 months.

To offset ongoing volume pressures, you ideally want FLXS to go slow on pricing upticks, but curtailing that looks challenging given the pressure they are facing from tariff surcharges. Cost pressures are now being felt at the operating level, with FLXS’s previously enticing operating margin progression contracting by 20 basis points in Q2 to 7.1%. What’s worse is that, based on management’s guidance for Q4, we could be staring at a near 200bps contraction in the margin profile of FLXS.

If one is looking for silver linings in the Q3 result, I suppose it would be the 40bps YoY improvement in gross margins to 22.6% (although it’s worth noting that FLXS’s gross margins are still a good deal lower than the home furnishing industry average of around 38%) mainly due to a favorable sales mix, which reflects well on some of the new product launches.

Another plus point also came through by way of solid free cash flow in Q3. As of H1-26 FCF stood at just a minuscule threshold of $2M, but the implied FCF (net cash through operating activities minus capital expenditure) of $23.6M suggests that free cash flow generation in just Q3 alone stood at a hefty $21.6M. Traditionally Q4 has always turned out to be a strong cash flow quarter, so investors can expect a similar cadence to Q3 (at the very least), taking the FY FCF position closer to the $50M levels.

Is FLXS Worth Pursuing Now?

At the outset of this section, I’d like to state that I remain quite conflicted about whether FLXS ought to be pursued now or not. Let me expand on my neutral stance by first starting with the valuation backdrop.

Prima facie, if you’re fishing for a cheap small-cap home furnishing stock, FLXS will likely pop up on your screener. At a forward earnings multiple of less than 12x it is over 20% cheaper than the average multiple of all of Haverty Furniture (HVT), Hooker Furnishings (HOFT), Bassett Furniture (BSET), Ethan Allen Interiors (ETD), and La-Z-Boy, Inc (LZB), which are home furnishers stocks within the small-cap market-cap range of $125M-$1500M. However, while FLXS is cheaper than its peers, its valuation multiple is now on par with its long-term average (also less than 12x),

Investors should also be aware that for an at-par multiple, FLXS is set to deliver lower forward earnings growth of -5.5% (this could potentially get worse, if FLXS provides weak guidance on the earnings call), while the average peerset earnings growth is close to 7%.

Some attention also ought to go towards the income profile of FLXS as it has been paying dividends for 36 years. While this is no doubt useful, note that relative to historical standards (over the past 5 years, the stock has yielded a figure of over 2.2%) it is quite poor. Crucially, the average of all these other small-cap home furnishers translates to a much superior figure of over 5% at their current prices!

Shifting to FLXS’s own charts, I am encouraged to see that FLXS no longer looks overextended to the upside like it did in February when the entire weekly candle was trading outside its upper bollinger band (already this band is a mark of overextension as it is two standard deviations higher than the 20-week rolling average). We’ve since seen a useful pullback to the 20-week moving average, with FLXS attempting to a build a base over the last 5 weeks. However, those with a conservative risk appetite could potentially argue that the $34-$37 levels would represent a better entry point as it coincides with the ascending trendline which has served as a support for 30 months now. A pullback to those levels would also coincide with the lower bollinger band which would validate selling overextension.

Finally, another reason why I feel that neutral positioning on FLXS would be apt now is because the stock’s relative strength ratio vs its small-cap peers from the consumer discretionary universe no longer looks oversold like it did in 2024, and is now roughly on par with its long-term mean of 0.44x.

To conclude, it’s fair to say that FLXS didn’t necessarily come out with the best set of results, or at least, results that could trigger a meaningful re-rating of the stock price. Coupled with that, FLXS’s mixed valuation picture, technical backdrop and income profile suggest that a neutral positioning would seem fair for now. All in all, I would rate FLXS as a hold.

data.title: Intuitive Machines: From Lunar Lander To Space Infrastructure Play
data.description:
Intuitive Machines (LUNR) is transitioning from a company that received its revenues from one-time contracts, to a more sustainable model with recurring revenue potential. This creates opportunity for long-term investors to buy a next-generation space company at a valuation where this potential isn't entirely priced in. With the win of the Near Space Network Services (NSNS) contract from NASA back in 2024, the company now also has infrastructure optionality. The contract has a total value of $4.82 billion and includes a five-year base period and a five-year option. It also positions them in the communications and navigation segment of the Artemis system instead of only being a transportation provider. This changes Intuitive's story from landing on the moon to developing and maintaining lunar communication, data relay services, and navigation, which has more long-term value.

Intuitive also has strong financials with $943 million in backlog and given guidance to nearly 5x revenue in 2026. In addition, the company provided information indicating that it expects to generate positive adjusted EBITDA during 2026, showing operating leverage as the NSNS project ramps up and other projects continue. The company also acquired Lanteris in early 2026 for $800 million, expanding Intuitive beyond lunar delivery services and allowing it to develop spacecraft and work in national security. NASA also awarded Intuitive a $180.4 million contract under IM-5 as well as a previously funded Lunar Terrain Vehicle contract worth $30 million, further solidifying the company in its role for lunar logistics and infrastructure. Overall, the company appears less speculative, with opportunities for recurring revenue and multiple ways to benefit from space exploration through both defense and commercial contracts. This is something I don't believe is fully factored into today's valuation, which is why I rate the stock a Buy.

The $4.82 Billion NSNS Contract

The key factor in the company's transformation is the NASA NSNS contract, awarded to Intuitive Machines in September of 2024 for up to $4.82 billion over a base term of five years with an additional five-year option. As opposed to other contracts for landing hardware, this contract is for infrastructure, where Intuitive is building a satellite constellation that will support lunar relay communication, navigation services, and data transmission for all of the NASA Artemis missions. A mission like this also paves the way for potential commercial and international missions, who could also use that infrastructure.

This changes Intuitive from a company earning money each time it successfully lands a spacecraft to one that will earn recurring service income so long as there are users for its infrastructure (which will be the case for years under the Artemis timeline). Whoever builds out the communication and navigation layer for lunar space will build the first lunar internet, making them the toll road for every mission after. With NASA looking for a long-term lunar base, this change should not be taken lightly. This is a key aspect I believe is being overlooked in today's valuation, where growth potential appears better than what the market is currently pricing in.

Revenue Updates

Intuitive Machines produced $210.1 million in FY 2025 revenue which represents a ~7.9% decline YoY. Additionally, the company achieved a 19% gross margin, along with a backlog of $943 million. Revenue guidance for FY 2026 appears strong, with a range of $0.9-1.0 billion, representing a near five-fold increase over last year's revenue. With ~65% of this guidance being supported by contracted backlog, it represents one of the most visible near-term ramps in the aerospace industry.

This large increase in revenue is expected to be driven by NSNS service revenue, Lanteris defense contributions, and continued delivery of CLPS missions. The company expects to report positive adjusted EBITDA for the first time in FY 2026, indicating that revenue is finally outpacing fixed costs. Expansion of gross margins to 19% in 2025 and projected positive adjusted EBITDA show that as service revenue grows there is operational leverage in this revenue expansion. This marks an inflection point for the company, where the pathway to profitability is becoming increasingly realistic.

Nova-D and the Heavy Lunar Logistics Market

Nova-D's ability to carry substantial payload weights places Intuitive at the forefront of contenders for the potential $4.6 billion Lunar Terrain Vehicle (LTV) contract. This LTV contract would provide for the delivery and operation of a crewed rover for the astronauts working on the lunar surface. The company was awarded a $30 million NASA contract as prime contractor for the LTV feasibility study. Winning this LTV contract would be transformative with respect to an already-full order book for the Company.

In March 2026, NASA selected Intuitive to receive a $180.4 million CLPS contract for the IM-5 mission that utilizes the Nova-D lander. This could help their odds for the large LTV contract, with their vehicles already being used by NASA. However, regardless of whether the LTV Contract is won, the Nova-D capability increases the addressable market for heavy scale lunar logistics significantly over the Odysseus-class lander. This provides optionality for the company, where if the $4.6 billion contract is won, it could potentially cause a re-rating in the company's valuation.

Defense Diversification

The $800 million completed purchase of Lanteris Space Systems in January 2026 added a national security component to the company that has the potential to alter the company's risk profile. The Lanteris 1300 series of spacecraft platforms will be able to provide cislunar space domain awareness and orbital maneuverability that directly aligns with the US Space Force's objective to monitor and operate in the space between the Earth and the Moon. The combined entity produced over $850 million in annual revenue and had a $920 million backlog as of the announcement date, while also maintaining positive adjusted EBITDA.

The Department of Defense (DoD) has begun treating cislunar space as a strategically important domain that requires a persistent presence, surveillance, and operational capability. With the acquisition, there is no other company that offers a vertically-integrated solution capable of delivering payload to the lunar surface while providing a cislunar space domain awareness platform. The DoD also represents a more durable customer base compared to customers reliant on NASA exploration budgets, which can sometimes be reduced due to government budget changes. This creates a more reliable stream of income for Intuitive, reducing a key source of risk for the company. This combined with upside from large NASA contract creates a favorable risk/reward for long-term investors, where potential growth may not be priced in.

Valuation

At a per share price of ~$28 and a ~$6 billion market cap, Intuitive trades ~4.7x forward sales. Established defense and space infrastructure prime contractors such as L3 Harris (LHX) and Booz Allen Hamilton (BAH) are trading at ~0.5-3.0x forward sales; however, both are mature, low-growth businesses. Defense tech high-growth companies with multi-billion dollar contract backlogs and strong forward revenue growth often see 5-10x forward revenue multiples. Intuitive is trading at the midpoint of these ranges, and it also guides to positive adjusted EBITDA, holds a $943 million backlog, and is part of multi-year US infrastructure mandates.

Applying a 7x forward revenue multiple on the midpoint of revenue guidance of $950 million brings the company's valuation to $7.6 billion. When divided by shares outstanding of ~218 million (calculated by dividing market cap by share price after acquisition completion), price per share is ~$34.94, which is my 12-month price target. I believe this is warranted as the backlog starts turning to revenue and the EBITDA inflection becomes reality. This also prices in the new risk profile of Intuitive, with steady defense revenues, and optionality for contracts like NASA's $4.6 billion LTV award.

Risks to the Thesis

Dilution is a risk, with Intuitive not yet profitable. Although positive adjusted EBITDA is expected in 2026, any delay in this timeline could lead to dilution.

With guidance expecting 5x in revenue YoY, execution is another risk. If Intuitive faces delays on schedules and milestones, it could cause them to miss estimates.

NASA budget pressure is probably the largest risk, with the Artemis program historically facing scheduling issues. Any change in this could affect Intuitive's contracts and how soon the revenue is realized. However, the DoD revenue should help offset this risk, which provides a steadier stream of cash than NASA contracts.

Takeaway

As Intuitive turns into an execution-driven story, it suggests that the company's opportunity is much larger and much more stable than ever before. It is now composed of communications infrastructure, heavy lunar logistics, and national security programs, which diversify its cash flows with revenue from more stable streams like defense. The NSNS contract also changes the business model to recurring revenue streams similar to those associated with infrastructure, changing the company's story to one that is not fully dependent on one-time contracts. These two elements provide a base for future success by providing less dependency on a single mission outcome and aligning itself with the long-term goals of the US in cislunar space.

If Intuitive Machines can execute on transitioning from backlog to sustainable revenue growth and demonstrate that their infrastructure strategy can scale, the stock could potentially command a re-rating. With a strong NASA partnership, the company has opportunity as a next-generation space business. With operations in key areas of exploration, communications, and defense, I see this as a strong possibility. This new mix of growth verticals makes Intuitive a compelling opportunity at current levels, which is why I'm rating the stock a Buy with a 12-month price target of $34.94.
